conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first read the official guidance to ensure I have the most accurate information for you.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. To help determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions.

First, do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child. Her name is Alex, and she's 4 years old. I live here in the UK and I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for her.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information! It's helpful to know about Alex.

To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask a few questions:

Does Alex live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, she lives with me at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's great. Just a few more questions to make sure I have all the information I need.

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for her.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Just to confirm a few final details:

Are you currently living in the UK, and is Alex also living in the UK with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right. We both live in the UK together.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you for confirming that. Just one more question to complete my assessment:

Do you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000 per year? (This won't affect whether you can claim Child Benefit, but it may affect whether there's a tax charge to pay.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of us has an income over £60,000.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Based on what you've told me, I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Let me provide you with my assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news!** You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Here's why:
- Alex is 4 years old, which is under the age limit of 16 (or 20 if in approved education/training)
- You live with Alex and are responsible for her care
- You both live in the UK
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex
- Only one person can claim Child Benefit per child, and since no one else is claiming, you can proceed with your claim

Additionally, since neither you nor your partner has an income over £60,000, you won't need to worry about the High Income Child Benefit tax charge.

You can go ahead and apply for Child Benefit. Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit or the application process?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 4 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 4 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 4 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit); Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit)
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 4 years old, which means she is under 16 and meets the age requirement for Child Benefit. You are responsible for Alex as she lives with you in the UK. No one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex, which is important as only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child. You live in the UK, which satisfies the residency requirement. Therefore, Alex meets all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 4), who lives with them in the UK. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. The claimant meets all the standard eligibility requirements: they are responsible for a child under 16, they live with the child, they reside in the UK, and no one else is claiming for the same child. Neither the claimant nor their partner has income over £60,000, so there would be no High Income Child Benefit tax charge to pay.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 4 years old, which means she is under 16 and meets the age requirement for Child Benefit. You are responsible for Alex as she lives with you in the UK. No one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex, which is important as only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child. You live in the UK, which satisfies the residency requirement. Therefore, Alex meets all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 4), who lives with them in the UK. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. The claimant meets all the standard eligibility requirements: they are responsible for a child under 16, they live with the child, they reside in the UK, and no one else is claiming for the same child. Neither the claimant nor their partner has income over £60,000, so there would be no High Income Child Benefit tax charge to pay.
